network.overall_strategy: This network is a literature-constrained hypothesis graph for prebiotic tryptophan centered on the documented bottleneck of indole generation and side-chain installation. It prioritizes the two strongest evidence lines: (1) hydrothermal Lost City-type serpentinization with Fe-rich saponite nanoconfinement, where indole-, skatole-, hydroxyanthranilate-, and tryptophan-like signals were reported, and (2) exogenous/interstellar indole formation from acetylene and ammonia. Because no fully replicated prebiotic terrestrial synthesis of tryptophan exists, the network explicitly marks unresolved links as speculative where required by connectivity. Convergence is organized around four hubs: indole, serine, alanine, and indole-3-acetaldehyde/tryptophan aminonitrile logic. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon and amino-acid precursors; surface chemistry concentrates, stabilizes, and photochemically diversifies feedstocks; biochemical/prebiotic settings provide confinement, hydrolysis, transamination, and aminonitrile conversion steps. The ten pathways are conditional variants through the same constrained evidence landscape rather than ten independently validated syntheses.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface CO2 activation followed by hydrogenation to formate on Fe-S phases.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fe-S catalysis of CO2 reduction to formate is foundational to alkaline vent carbon fixation models and is consistent with vent chimney mineralogy and geochemical plausibility (Russell & Hall, 1997; Martin & Russell, 2007; White et al., 2015; Santos-Carballal et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reduced-carbon diversification to formaldehyde and pyruvate-equivalent pool

conditions.other: strongly reducing, subseafloor/alkaline vent interface; network-bridging abstraction of vent carbon fixation products

reactions.mechanism: Successive CO2/formate reduction and C-C bond-forming vent chemistry generate a reduced carbon pool feeding aldehydes and alpha-keto acids.
reactions.reasoning: Direct one-step experimental conversion to both formaldehyde and pyruvate is not established; this node is a conservative network abstraction representing hydrothermal reduced-carbon output that underpins prebiotic alpha-keto acid chemistry. It is included strictly to connect literature-supported vent fixation chemistry to downstream amino acid and sugar precursor chemistry; pyruvate formation from hydrothermal CO2/H2 systems is discussed in vent-origin frameworks, while amino acid formation from CO2/NH4+/H2 becomes thermodynamically favorable at hydrothermal conditions (Amend & Shock, 1998; Russell & Hall, 1997; Martin & Russell, 2007).

reactions.name: Reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine
reactions.mechanism: Surface-mediated reductive amination of an alpha-keto acid to the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids on sulfide minerals is consistent with hydrothermal amino acid synthesis models; pyrite photochemical reductive amination and Fe-S catalysis support alpha-keto acid to amino acid conversion under plausible prebiotic conditions (Amend & Shock, 1998; Nature Communications, 2024; White et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface UV generation and concentration of HCN/formamide cyanide pool

conditions.other: strong UV, evaporitic concentration, anoxic surface pools

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical and dehydration chemistry generates a reactive nitrile/amide pool including HCN and formamide from simple C1 and nitrogen feedstocks.
reactions.reasoning: This is a necessary connecting step rather than a single directly demonstrated reaction under the exact stoichiometry shown. The literature supports central roles for HCN and formamide in prebiotic surface chemistry and UV-driven networks, while TiO2 catalyzes formamide-derived heterocycle chemistry and sulfide photoredox systems support cyanosulfidic chemistry. Included as a cautious feedstock-pool abstraction, not as a validated dedicated HCN synthesis experiment for tryptophan (Patel et al., 2015; Saladino et al., 2012; Saladino et al., 2003).

reactions.name: Borate-stabilized formose entry to glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: evaporitic pools, wet-dry cycling, alkaline episodes

reactions.mechanism: Formose-type aldol network produces small sugars; borate stabilizes pentose/triose intermediates and suppresses decomposition.
reactions.reasoning: Glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde are established products/intermediates in formaldehyde-derived sugar networks, although selectivity is poor. This is represented as a pooled output rather than an unrealistically single clean step, addressing prior over-compression concerns (Butlerow, 1861; Breslow, 1959; cited in the research summary for formose chemistry and sugar precursor generation).

reactions.name: Strecker-type serine synthesis from glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: evaporitic pool concentration followed by hydrolytic workup

reactions.mechanism: Aldehyde imination with ammonia, cyanide addition to aminonitrile, then hydrolysis to the amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Serine is one of the more plausible prebiotic amino acids and is discussed in the literature as accessible from glycolaldehyde or glyceraldehyde through Strecker logic; the displayed reaction compresses aminonitrile formation and hydrolysis into one network node for tractability (Kirschning, 2022; Li et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Alternative Strecker-type serine synthesis from glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: wet-dry cycles, concentration in shallow pools

reactions.mechanism: Aldehyde-to-aminonitrile Strecker sequence followed by hydrolysis to serine.
reactions.reasoning: The literature explicitly notes glycolaldehyde or glyceraldehyde as plausible serine precursors in prebiotic chemistry. This provides a second convergent route to the serine hub (Kirschning, 2022; Li et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyrrole synthesis from acetylene and ammonia

conditions.catalyst: sulfide mineral hydrothermal analog; literature specifically used NiS/CoS
conditions.other: anaerobic volcanic-hydrothermal setting; network uses Fe-sulfide analogs to satisfy schema while noting literature metal sulfides

reactions.mechanism: Acetylene-ammonia coupling on metal sulfide surfaces gives N-heterocycle formation.
reactions.reasoning: Pyrrole formation from acetylene and ammonia under aqueous anaerobic catalytic conditions is experimentally supported, though the published catalysts were NiS and CoS rather than Fe-only phases. This reaction is included because pyrrole is one of the few documented prebiotic heteroaromatic precursors relevant to indole chemistry (Seel et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Indole delivery/availability node from exogenous and hydrothermal sources

conditions.pressure: mixed: extraterrestrial low-pressure source plus hydrothermal emplacement
conditions.catalyst: iron-rich saponite as concentrator; interstellar energetic processing for source formation
conditions.other: represents two evidence lines: exogenous indole formation and natural hydrothermal occurrence; terrestrial de novo indole synthesis remains unverified

reactions.mechanism: Network convergence node for indole availability through exogenous delivery and/or unresolved hydrothermal generation in serpentinization environments.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is intentionally marked as a source-availability abstraction rather than a validated terrestrial annulation reaction. Indole was directly observed from acetylene/ammonia interstellar ice chemistry, and indole was also detected in Atlantis Massif serpentinites associated with putative abiotic organosynthesis. No prebiotic Earth laboratory route from pyrrole to indole is known, so this node avoids claiming a solved mechanism (Kaiser et al., 2024; Ménez et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Fe-rich saponite nanoconfined hydrothermal tryptophan-forming branch

conditions.other: alkaline pH 9-11, serpentinization-derived H2/NH3, reducing nanoconfined microreactors, Lost City-type system

reactions.mechanism: Fe-rich clay nanoconfinement promotes aromatic side-chain installation/Friedel-Crafts-like chemistry and associated aromatic amino-acid precursor formation.
reactions.reasoning: This is the core literature-grounded but unreplicated hydrothermal hypothesis. Ménez et al. reported abiotic tryptophan together with indole, skatole, and hydroxyanthranilate in iron-rich saponite nanopores from serpentinites and proposed Fe-saponite-catalyzed Friedel-Crafts alkylation under alkaline reducing hydrothermal conditions. Because the exact sequence is unresolved, this node outputs tryptophan and two observed/related intermediates rather than asserting a fully defined stepwise mechanism (Ménez et al., 2018; Proskurowski et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Speculative formation of indole-3-acetaldehyde from indole in confined prebiotic media

conditions.other: concentrated interfaces, mineral confinement or membrane partitioning; explicitly speculative

reactions.mechanism: Hypothetical side-chain oxidation/extension from indole to the corresponding aldehyde precursor.
reactions.reasoning: Kirschning (2022) explicitly states that Strecker synthesis from indole-3-acetaldehyde is theoretically sound but that this aldehyde has no known prebiotic source and is unstable. This node is therefore deliberately labeled speculative and included only because the literature identifies indole-3-acetaldehyde as the key theoretical Strecker precursor to tryptophan (Kirschning, 2022).

reactions.name: Strecker-type tryptophan aminonitrile formation

conditions.other: evaporitic or shoreline pools, anoxic, cyanide-rich

reactions.mechanism: Canonical Strecker addition to an aldehyde gives the alpha-aminonitrile corresponding to tryptophan.
reactions.reasoning: The literature identifies this route as chemically sensible in principle: indole-3-acetaldehyde + HCN + NH3 gives a tryptophan aminonitrile precursor. The step is included because it is the best-defined bottom-up route once the aldehyde exists, even though its precursor source remains unresolved (Kirschning, 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of tryptophan aminonitrile to tryptophan

conditions.other: geothermal cycling or prolonged aqueous hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydration and hydrolysis convert the aminonitrile to the alpha-amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of aminonitriles to amino acids is standard Strecker follow-up chemistry and is the most defensible late-stage conversion in a hypothetical tryptophan network. Included as the least speculative portion of the indole-3-acetaldehyde route (Kirschning, 2022).

pathways.name: Lost City direct hydrothermal saponite route from alanine-supported precursor pool
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon fixation yields pyruvate and alanine; indole availability converges in Fe-rich saponite nanopores where the Ménez-type hydrothermal branch gives tryptophan.

pathways.name: Lost City direct hydrothermal saponite route with serine branch
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced carbon feeds surface sugar chemistry and Strecker serine synthesis; indole and serine re-enter hydrothermal nanoconfined Fe-saponite settings that yield tryptophan-like products.

pathways.name: Alternative serine-entry hydrothermal saponite route
pathways.description: As in P2, but serine derives from glyceraldehyde rather than glycolaldehyde before convergence with indole in hydrothermal clay nanopores.

pathways.name: Acetylene-to-pyrrole-assisted indole availability plus hydrothermal saponite branch
pathways.description: Hydrothermal acetylene and ammonia produce pyrrole; indole availability is treated as unresolved but supported by exogenous and natural hydrothermal occurrence; final tryptophan formation follows the Ménez scenario.

pathways.name: Exogenous indole plus hydrothermal alanine nanoconfinement route
pathways.description: Indole is supplied by exogenous or unresolved hydrothermal sources; hydrothermal alanine production and Fe-rich saponite nanoconfinement yield tryptophan directly in the natural-setting hypothesis.

pathways.name: Exogenous indole plus glycolaldehyde-Strecker to aminonitrile route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced carbon feeds HCN and glycolaldehyde production; a speculative indole-3-acetaldehyde precursor is then converted through the literature-theoretical Strecker aminonitrile route to tryptophan.

pathways.name: Exogenous indole plus glyceraldehyde-assisted aldehyde precursor route
pathways.description: A second variant of the Strecker endgame using the same unresolved aromatic aldehyde precursor but a different sugar-entry branch upstream.

pathways.name: Pyrrole-enabled indole availability followed by Strecker endgame
pathways.description: Pyrrole from acetylene/ammonia feeds the indole hub; once indole-3-acetaldehyde is admitted as a speculative precursor, Strecker aminonitrile chemistry leads to tryptophan.

pathways.name: Serine-rich pool with indole hydrothermal re-entry
pathways.description: Surface synthesis of serine from glycolaldehyde converges with hydrothermal indole availability and Fe-saponite nanoconfinement to produce tryptophan in the strongest natural-setting hypothesis.

pathways.name: Dual-hub integrated route comparing hydrothermal and Strecker terminal solutions
pathways.description: This integrated pathway passes through all major hubs and emphasizes that the field currently has two conditional terminal solutions: the natural-setting Fe-saponite route and the theoretical indole-3-acetaldehyde Strecker route. The listed sequence follows the latter for graph completeness.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest literature-facing route because it uses Lost City-style hydrothermal confinement and the Ménez et al. natural occurrence evidence. However, the core weakness remains severe: rxn_009 assumes hydrothermal indole availability from pyrrole/acetylene/ammonia without experimental terrestrial demonstration, and rxn_010 compresses multiple unresolved transformations into one node. The pathway is coherent as a hypothesis graph but not as an experimentally validated synthesis.
suggestions: Separate the direct saponite branch into explicit substeps reflecting the Ménez hypothesis rather than a single omnibus reaction
Downgrade terrestrial pyrrole-to-indole conversion to clearly exogenous indole input unless experimental support is available
Use Lost City temperature range closer to 313-363K (40-90C) or at most <473K for the specific saponite setting

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway improves environmental interconnection by using surface serine generation before hydrothermal re-entry, consistent with literature that serine is prebiotically accessible. But reintroducing surface-generated serine into a hydrothermal tryptophan-forming branch still depends on unresolved indole formation and an unreplicated Fe-saponite Friedel-Crafts step. Plausible as a network bridge, weak as a demonstrated route.
suggestions: Specify survival and transport rationale for serine from surface pools into protected hydrothermal microenvironments
Represent serine synthesis via aminonitrile and hydrolysis steps rather than one-step Strecker-to-serine
Keep indole source exogenous unless direct hydrothermal annulation data are cited

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The alternative glyceraldehyde-serine entry is weaker than the glycolaldehyde route because the listed one-step transformation to serine is chemically oversimplified and less clearly grounded. The same major bottlenecks remain: no demonstrated prebiotic terrestrial indole annulation and no replicated direct hydrothermal tryptophan synthesis from indole plus serine/alanine equivalents.
suggestions: Replace direct glyceraldehyde-to-serine with aminonitrile intermediate accounting
Reduce confidence in glyceraldehyde route due to formose selectivity issues
Explicitly model confinement because free indole and open-water serine condensation is kinetically unfavorable

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Pyrrole-assisted indole availability is literature-aware because pyrrole from acetylene plus ammonia over sulfides is supported. But the network overextends this evidence into indole production under hydrothermal terrestrial conditions, which the supplied literature explicitly identifies as unproven. The endpoint still leans heavily on the Ménez interpretation without laboratory replication.
suggestions: Treat pyrrole as a supported precursor but not evidence for prebiotic terrestrial indole synthesis
Add a branch distinguishing exogenous indole delivery from in situ hydrothermal generation
Clarify whether alanine acts as side-chain donor in a Friedel-Crafts-like step or is only correlative with detected products

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Among all routes this is the best aligned with the research landscape because it avoids claiming a terrestrial de novo indole synthesis and instead assumes exogenous indole delivery, which is supported by Kaiser et al. The problem is that the terminal hydrothermal coupling to tryptophan remains unreplicated and mechanistically compressed. Still, this is the least speculative route in the current network.
suggestions: Make exogenous indole an explicit starting node rather than embedding it in a hydrothermal reaction
Break rxn_010 into indole adsorption, side-chain installation, and final amino acid release
Address indole partitioning/escape by explicitly invoking nanoconfinement in Fe-rich saponite pores

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a relatively coherent hybrid route: supported hydrothermal reduced carbon, plausible surface cyanide chemistry, serine-accessible chemistry, and a literature-supported theoretical Strecker endgame from indole-3-acetaldehyde. Its central defect is rxn_011: there is no established prebiotic source of indole-3-acetaldehyde, and the user network correctly labels this speculative. Once that intermediate is granted, the terminal Strecker/hydrolysis logic is reasonable.
suggestions: Explicitly mark indole-3-acetaldehyde generation as the dominant bottleneck
Consider using delivered or photochemically oxidized indole derivatives only if supported by experiments
Add an aminonitrile intermediate for serine formation to improve mechanistic realism

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route shares the same strengths and weaknesses as P6 but uses the weaker glyceraldehyde-serine branch. The aminonitrile endgame is chemically sensible if indole-3-acetaldehyde exists, but the precursor chemistry is less robust and the direct glyceraldehyde-to-serine representation is weak.
suggestions: Prefer glycolaldehyde-based serine precursor chemistry over glyceraldehyde for stronger support
Represent sugar interconversion and Strecker sequence more explicitly
Do not imply demonstrated aqueous formation of indole-3-acetaldehyde from indole

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is concise but highly dependent on two unsupported steps in series: pyrrole-to-indole annulation on early Earth and indole-to-indole-3-acetaldehyde formation. The Strecker endgame itself is the most orthodox chemistry in the network; the challenge is that both prerequisites are missing from the experimental literature.
suggestions: Replace rxn_009 with explicit exogenous indole delivery if seeking literature fidelity
Investigate whether aldehyde-functionalized indole derivatives can be supplied exogenously rather than formed in situ
Keep this route labeled as a speculative completion rather than evidence-based pathway

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The network logic here is good: serine-rich surface pools feed a confined hydrothermal endgame. This cross-environment strategy fits the requested design principles. Yet chemically it still hinges on unsupported indole generation and an unreplicated direct hydrothermal tryptophan-forming branch. Good topology, limited empirical grounding.
suggestions: Explicitly justify how serine survives transport and avoids UV degradation
Use exogenous indole or detected natural indole as a separate availability node instead of synthetic claim
Provide narrower microenvironment conditions for Fe-saponite nanoconfinement

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the best systems-level pathway because it explicitly compares the two major endgames in the literature landscape: the Ménez hydrothermal branch and the theoretical Strecker branch from indole-3-acetaldehyde. It excellently captures convergence and environmental interconnection. However, as chemistry it compounds the two major bottlenecks rather than resolving them: terrestrial indole formation and prebiotic indole-3-acetaldehyde generation.
suggestions: This pathway would benefit from explicit uncertainty annotation on all bottleneck nodes
Separate evidence-backed branches from speculative connectors more sharply
Add literature citations and exact mineral/condition details per reaction to improve alignment and evaluability

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically solid and commendably literature-aware about the central unsolved nature of prebiotic tryptophan synthesis. It includes enough molecules and reactions, identifies sensible hubs, and provides multiple convergent routes centered on indole, serine, alanine, and aminonitrile logic. Environmental interconnection is a major strength: hydrothermal reduced-carbon chemistry feeds surface cyanide/sugar chemistry, and surface products feed biochemical or hydrothermal endgames. However, the network falls well short of approval because its key target-forming steps rest on precisely the unresolved gaps highlighted in the supplied literature. The largest issues are: (1) rxn_009 effectively asserts terrestrial hydrothermal indole formation from pyrrole/acetylene/ammonia, despite the literature stating this has not been demonstrated; (2) rxn_010 conflates natural detection of tryptophan in Fe-rich saponite with a mechanistically validated synthetic reaction, compressing multiple unknown transformations into one step; (3) rxn_011 proposes indole-3-acetaldehyde formation without experimental prebiotic support, though this speculation is at least acknowledged; and (4) several reactions oversimplify known chemistry, especially direct Strecker-to-serine and formate-to-formaldehyde/pyruvate pooling. Literature alignment is mixed: the network correctly emphasizes Ménez et al. 2018 and Kaiser et al. 2024, and it respects the statement that no complete replicated prebiotic synthesis exists, but it still overstates unsupported transformations in order to maintain graph connectivity. A further formal issue is that the provided summary does not state mineral agents and per-reaction citations explicitly, which were required and are important for evaluating environmental realism. Overall, this is a good hypothesis network and a fair map of the current evidence landscape, but not a high-confidence viable synthesis network to tryptophan.
